title: Why Does Losing $100 Hurt More Than Finding $100 Feels Good?
summary: Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky's prospect theory and loss aversion in behavioral economics and psychology, plus the 2018 challenge from David Gal and Derek Rucker and capuchin monkey experiments by Laurie Santos.
answer: In 1979, Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky's prospect theory found that people evaluate money relative to a reference point, and that the psychological impact of losses follows a steeper curve than equivalent gains -- losses hurt more than the same-sized gain feels good. Estimates put the effect at roughly 1.5 to 2.5 times as strong, and it shows up in gambling behavior, in capuchin monkeys trading tokens for food, and in fMRI scans where reward-related brain activity drops off more sharply for potential losses than it rises for potential gains. A later, still-ongoing academic debate found the effect is real but more context-dependent than the popular version suggests, showing up more reliably at larger stakes than small ones.
key_findings: In 1979, Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky's prospect theory found people evaluate money relative to a reference point, with losses producing a steeper psychological reaction than equivalent gains.
Later estimates put loss aversion at roughly 1.5 to 2.5 times as psychologically strong as an equivalent gain, with 2x as the figure most commonly cited.
Capuchin monkeys trading tokens for food showed the same lopsided sensitivity to losses versus equivalent gains -- a hint the asymmetry predates human economic culture.

fMRI scans during gambling decisions found reward-related brain activity rose with potential gains but dropped more steeply with potential losses -- and the steepness individually predicted each person's own loss-averse behavior.

A 2018 paper directly challenged loss aversion as a universal law; even researchers who disagreed conceded it's more context-dependent than commonly taught, showing up more reliably at larger stakes than small ones.
article_markdown:
## Two feelings that should be mirror images

Imagine you find a hundred dollars on the sidewalk -- genuinely unexpected, free money. You'd feel good about that for a while. Now imagine instead you reach into your pocket and the hundred dollars you had is just gone. Same amount, same effect on your bank balance, in the opposite direction. If you're honest, those two feelings aren't mirror images. Losing the hundred dollars would probably bother you longer and more than finding it would have delighted you.

## A dollar is supposed to be a dollar

That should bother you a little as a strictly rational question. Gaining a hundred dollars and losing a hundred dollars are the same event running in opposite directions -- a purely logical calculator of self-interest should feel exactly as much about one as the other, just flipped in sign. Classical economic theory assumed exactly that for a long time. Real people don't do that, and the asymmetry isn't subtle or rare -- it shows up in gambling decisions, negotiations, and something as mundane as deciding whether to sell a possession you already own.

## Prospect theory

In 1979, psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky published a paper that would become the single most-cited paper in the history of the journal Econometrica. They called their model prospect theory, and it directly challenged the assumption that people evaluate money based on total final wealth. Their finding was that people evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point -- usually wherever they currently stand -- treating anything better as a gain and anything worse as a loss from that point.

That reframing alone doesn't explain the asymmetry. What does is how the psychological impact of gains and losses behaves as the numbers get bigger: gains feel good with diminishing returns, but the whole loss side of the curve is steeper. A loss of a given size produces a stronger psychological reaction than a gain of the exact same size. That steeper slope is loss aversion.

## Putting a number on it

Later researchers estimated how much steeper -- somewhere between one and a half and two and a half times as strong, with two times as the figure that ended up in most textbooks. Under that estimate, losing a hundred dollars carries roughly the psychological weight of gaining two hundred.

This shows up well beyond hypothetical gambling questions. It's behind the endowment effect -- people demanding more money to give up something they already own than they'd pay to acquire the identical item fresh, because giving it up registers as a loss while acquiring it registers only as a gain. It's behind sticking with a default option, because switching can register as risking a loss even when the alternative is objectively better. It's behind refusing to sell a bad investment, because selling at a loss makes the loss final in a way holding onto it doesn't.

## Older than money itself?

In a 2006 study, researchers trained capuchin monkeys to trade small tokens for food, giving the monkeys a working currency. When the setup was adjusted so a monkey occasionally experienced what functioned as a loss, the monkeys showed the same lopsided sensitivity -- reacting more strongly to a loss than an equivalent gain. A species that never developed money or economic theory showing the same asymmetry is a hint the tendency runs deeper than culture alone.

There's a neural signature too. A 2007 study scanned people's brains while they decided whether to accept a series of 50/50 gambles. As the potential gain increased, activity rose in reward-related brain regions including the ventral striatum -- but as the potential loss increased, activity in those same regions decreased. The asymmetry was individually predictive: people whose brain activity dropped off more steeply for potential losses were the same people who behaved more loss-averse when actually deciding whether to take the gamble.

## A live disagreement worth taking seriously

In 2018, researchers David Gal and Derek Rucker published a pointedly titled paper: "The Loss of Loss Aversion: Will It Loom Larger Than Its Gain?" Their argument wasn't that losses never feel worse than gains -- it was that the evidence for loss aversion as a broad, universal law is weaker and more context-dependent than the popular version suggests.

Other researchers pushed back on specific pieces of that argument, but even those who disagreed largely conceded the underlying point: loss aversion had been accepted too uncritically as a fixed, universal law. Later work looking specifically at stake size found the effect shows up more reliably at larger amounts and becomes less consistent -- sometimes disappearing entirely -- at smaller ones. A hundred dollars sits closer to the small end of that range, meaning the classic "twice as bad" framing might be least reliable exactly where it's most commonly illustrated.

## What actually holds up

For a lot of people, in a lot of situations, losing a hundred dollars really does feel worse than finding a hundred dollars feels good, and the asymmetry Kahneman and Tversky identified remains one of the most influential findings in behavioral science. But the honest answer isn't "always, exactly twice as much." It's closer to: often, unevenly, and more reliably at higher stakes than the textbook example usually illustrates. The interesting finding was never the exact number -- it was the discovery that "a dollar is a dollar" isn't actually how anyone experiences money.

sources.title: Kahneman, D. & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk. Econometrica, 47(2), 263-291.
sources.publisher: Prospect theory -- reference-point-dependent evaluation; value function steeper for losses than gains

sources.title: The Sveriges Riksbank Prize in Economic Sciences in Memory of Alfred Nobel 2002 -- awarded to Daniel Kahneman (shared with Vernon L. Smith) "for having integrated insights from psychological research into economic science."
sources.publisher: Kahneman awarded 2002 Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences for prospect theory work; Tversky ineligible (died 1996, prize not awarded posthumously)

sources.title: Tversky, A. & Kahneman, D. (1992). Advances in Prospect Theory: Cumulative Representation of Uncertainty. Journal of Risk and Uncertainty, 5(4), 297-323.
sources.publisher: Loss-aversion coefficient (λ) estimates cluster 1.5-2.5, canonical ~2.25 value used in most textbook treatments

sources.title: Kahneman, D., Knetsch, J.L. & Thaler, R.H. (1991). Anomalies: The Endowment Effect, Loss Aversion, and Status Quo Bias. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 5(1), 193-206.
sources.publisher: Endowment effect, status quo bias, and related anomalies as downstream phenomena of loss aversion

sources.title: Chen, M.K., Lakshminarayanan, V. & Santos, L.R. (2006). How Basic Are Behavioral Biases? Evidence from Capuchin Monkey Trading Behavior. Journal of Political Economy, 114(3), 517-537.
sources.publisher: Capuchin monkeys show loss-averse trading behavior with token currency, suggesting the asymmetry predates human economic culture

sources.title: Gal, D. & Rucker, D.D. (2018). The Loss of Loss Aversion: Will It Loom Larger Than Its Gain? Journal of Consumer Psychology, 28(3), 497-516.
sources.publisher: Required hedge -- direct challenge to loss aversion's universality as a general psychological law, arguing current evidence doesn't support losses being more impactful than gains across the board

sources.title: Simonson, I. & Kivetz, R. (2018). Bringing (Contingent) Loss Aversion Down to Earth -- A Comment on Gal & Rucker's Rejection of "Losses Loom Larger Than Gains." Journal of Consumer Psychology, 28(3), 517-522.
sources.publisher: Response to Gal & Rucker -- pushed back on their overall conclusion but conceded loss aversion had been accepted too uncritically as a fixed, universal law rather than something context-dependent

sources.title: Yechiam, E. (2019). Acceptable Losses: The Debatable Origins of Loss Aversion. Psychological Research, 83(7), 1327-1339.
sources.publisher: Loss aversion effect is more reliable at larger stakes and becomes less consistent, sometimes absent, at smaller stakes -- directly relevant to the episode's own $100 example

sources.title: Tom, S.M., Fox, C.R., Trepel, C. & Poldrack, R.A. (2007). The Neural Basis of Loss Aversion in Decision-Making Under Risk. Science, 315(5811), 515-518.
sources.publisher: Neural basis of loss aversion: fMRI during 50/50 gambling decisions showed activity in gain-sensitive brain regions (including ventral striatum) increased with potential gain size but decreased with potential loss size; the steepness of this neural asymmetry individually predicted each participant's own behavioral loss aversion

seo.title: Why Does Losing $100 Hurt More Than Finding $100 Feels Good? | Hardwired Stickman
seo.description: Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky's prospect theory and loss aversion in behavioral economics and psychology, plus the 2018 challenge from David Gal and Derek Rucker and capuchin monkey experiments by Laurie Santos.

hooks: Why Does Losing $100 Hurt More Than Finding It Feels Good?
Even Monkeys Show This Money Bias
Your Brain Weighs Losses Twice as Heavily
pinterest.description: Nobel-winning research found losses hurt roughly twice as much as equivalent gains feel good -- and the effect shows up in monkey brains and fMRI scans too. An illustrated, research-backed explainer on loss aversion.

instagram.caption: Losing $100 and finding $100 should feel like mirror images. They don't. Kahneman and Tversky's Nobel-winning research found losses hit roughly twice as hard -- and even capuchin monkeys show the same bias. The full explanation is on Hardwired Stickman.

slides.heading: WHY DOES LOSING $100 HURT MORE THAN FINDING $100 FEELS GOOD?
slides.body: Mathematically identical. Emotionally, not even close.

slides.heading: THE OBVIOUS EXPLANATION
slides.body: A dollar is a dollar. Classical economics assumed exactly that -- and was wrong.

slides.heading: PROSPECT THEORY, 1979
slides.body: Kahneman and Tversky found losses hit roughly twice as hard as equivalent gains feel good.

slides.heading: EVEN MONKEYS SHOW IT
slides.body: Capuchin monkeys trading tokens for food showed the exact same lopsided bias toward losses.

slides.heading: IT SHOWS UP ON A BRAIN SCAN
slides.body: fMRI scans found reward-region activity drops off sharper for losses than it rises for gains.

slides.heading: BUT IT'S NOT AS SIMPLE AS 'ALWAYS 2X'
slides.body: Later research found the effect is real but more reliable at high stakes than small ones.

slides.heading: WATCH THE FULL EXPLANATION
slides.body: Hardwired Stickman -- why humans and animals behave the way they do.

reel.hook: Losing $100 and finding $100 should feel equal. They don't -- and there's a reason.
beats: Show the stickman finding $100, then losing $100 -- compare reactions.
Reveal Kahneman and Tversky's Nobel-winning prospect theory.
Explain even capuchin monkeys show the same bias trading tokens for food.
Close on the brain scan: losses hit reward circuitry harder than gains lift it.
